allocations.rationale: Since you're conservative and want a bit of stability, 10% in Gold is perfect. Gold doesn't go up and down like stocks. It usually moves up slowly and stays there, helping to protect your money when stock markets crash. Plus, it's taxed only when you sell, same as your equity funds.

fund.name: Kotak Multi Asset Allocation Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: This is the only 'safer options' fund we can use for you. Normally, debt funds attract full tax on your earnings. But this special fund combines gold, stocks, and debt in small amounts, which makes it 'look' like an equity fund for tax purposes. It usually just goes up and never goes too far down, giving you the comfort you need without the tax pain.

fund.name: ICICI Prudential Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: This is your core 'safe-growing' fund. It mixes mostly stocks with some bonds. Because it has more than 65% in stocks, it's taxed the same way as pure stock funds (much lighter tax), even though it has some bonds to keep it steady. It's been around for 26 years and is very consistent. Good for long-term savings.

fund.name: Nippon India Multi Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: This is your main wealth builder. Multi-cap funds pick stocks from big, medium, and small companies. Over long periods like yours (25 years), these smaller companies grow faster. It's taxed lightly like all other stock funds. It has 21 years of history and a large AUM, so it's managed by experienced folks.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Flexi-cap funds decide how much to put in big or small companies based on what's cheap in the market right now. EDF has already built up enough stock wealth, so adding a second fund that can shift its focus keeps you safe if one type of stock underperforms. This one has 31 years of history and a huge amount of money invested in it.

plan.setup_phase:
Step 1: Open a 'Direct' Mutual Fund Account on Kuvera, Groww, or Coin (FIRST time users only can do this easily). Don't use 'Regular' plans — they charge extra yearly fees that eat your returns over 25 years.

Step 2: Total ₹1,21,000 goes into SIPs. Set up automatic monthly INSTRUCTIONS for these 5 funds. Our advice is to start with the 'Growth' option (money stays invested) for long-term goals. If you want to change quickly for mid-term needs like the home, choose the 'LID' (next day) option instead.

Step 3: Keep ₹5.5 Lakhs (your existing corpus) in these funds as a 'starter base'. Since ₹5.5L is small compared to future needs, we'll let it grow slowly without pushing too hard for high returns right now.

Step 4: Check that first few SIPs went through by checking your app after 15 days. If everything is working, don't touch it. Let the magic happen.
review_checkpoints:
After 6 months: Just look at your net worth. Has it gone up even a little? SIPs need time to work. If not, check if your money is actually being invested or stuck in bank.

After 1 year: Look at the 'Large Industry' chart in your app. If any fund looks much smaller than the other funds, or if your portfolio now has 70% in one place, don't panic. Just wait. At Year 6 (when you need the house), we will rebalance more carefully.

After 3 years: Check if your life changed. If you got a bonus, save it. If payments changed (salary, taxes), adjust the SIPs. Keep the plan simple.

Once a year (or at the year-end), look at your portfolio. Did your stock portion grow to 80% while we wanted 60%? If so, slowly move some money out of the 'active' funds into the 'Multi Asset Allocation' fund (the safer one). This happens again at Year 1 (home need) and Year 6 (big house need), when we shift more into the safe fund to protect the money we need soon.
21.4% (This is the middle path, made of: 50% stocks returning 13% + 10% gold returning 10% + 40% 'safe' fund returning 7% averaged over a full lifetime. It means your 8 Cr goal is achievable.)
29.8% (In a great market with all stocks going up, you could earn this. It means even small changes compound fast over 25 years.)
13.0% (In a bad market where everything drops and takes longer to recover, you'd earn less. But with 25 years left, you can survive this and still retire well.)

You're 35, which is perfect for investing. You have 25 years until retirement, giving your money enough time to grow very big. You're 'conservative' so we kept 45% of your money in 'safer' options that barely go down, but because you're a 'new regime' tax investor (paying flat 30% tax every year regardless of how long you hold), using normal debt funds for long-term growth cost you a lot in taxes. That's why we use ETF Gold and Special Multi-Asset funds — they protect your money and cost less in tax than you'd think. The 30% in multi-cap and 20% in flexi-cap are your money-makers. Even if the market goes up 40% in a good year or down 20% in a bad one, over 25 years, you'll build your 8 Cr goal.

Markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year — your portfolio value will temporarily go down. This is normal and recovers over time.

Inflation usually eats away your purchasing power — so 8 crores feels different in 40 years than today.

Over-optimistic goals — expecting 29.8% returns (the 'bull' market) might leave you short if the market stays normal or rough.

Never invade your 'core wealth' during your lifetime. Keep these funds untouched. You can borrow (with zero interest on your loans) if you need to upgrade your house.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice. Ask a certified advisor to double-check before investing.

aps.reasoning: 1. passive_instrument_fraction (0.4): The plan allocates 10% to Gold (passive) and relies heavily on actively managed hybrid/equity funds for tax efficiency. However, one core equity allocation (20% Flexi Cap) is automatically passive due to manager discretion following valuation, bringing the equity passive fraction to 40%.

pqs.reasoning:
Goal Alignment (0.4): The plan assigns a 25-year, retirement-focused portfolio (75% equity) to an investor with a 'conservative' risk appetite. While the IRA and child education goals have long time horizons, the home upgrade goal is in 6 years—funding this with volatile equity funds is highly misaligned with a conservative risk profile. Additionally, for a conservative investor, a 10% allocation to a Gold ETF is disproportionately large; gold has high volatility and is generally considered a strategic hedge (3-5%), not a core stability component.

Diversification (0.9): The plan offers excellent diversification across asset classes and geographies. It includes a Gold ETF, a Multi-Cap fund, a Flexi-Cap fund, an Aggressive Hybrid, and a Multi-Asset Allocation fund (claims to cover gold, stocks, debt). It also diversifies fund houses (UTI, Nippon, HDFC, ICICI).

Risk Return Appropriateness (0.3): The portfolio is severely mismatched for a conservative investor. With 75% in equity-oriented funds (Flexi, Multi-Cap, Aggressive Hybrid) and 10% in volatile Gold, the plan exposes the investor to significant drawdowns (potentially 20-30%+) that a conservative risk appetite cannot tolerate, especially given the 6-year horizon for the home purchase. The projected returns rely heavily on aggressive equity growth.

Internal Consistency (0.5): The plan contains a major contradiction regarding tax efficiency. It explicitly states the investor is a 'new regime' tax investor in the rationale (claiming flat 30% tax applies), yet claims to optimize by using equity wrappers to 'save on tax.' Under the new regime, equity funds and debt funds are taxed at the same marginal slab rates; there is no tax advantage to using equity wrappers for debt needs. The plan invents a tax benefit that does not exist, contradicting the reality of the customer's profile.

Tax Efficiency (0.2): Since the investor is in the 'new_regime', the plan should generally focus on fund selection quality as tax is not a differentiator between debt and equity. However, the plan catastrophically fails here by claiming to save taxes for a new-regime investor using a justification that applies only to the old regime (80C/LT CG benefits). It misrepresents the tax landscape completely. Furthermore, for a conservative investor, the plan exposes them to heavy tax on debt-like losses in equity funds, ignoring the principle that conservative funds should typically use slab-taxed wrappers in the old regime. While 80C is irrelevant for this investor, the specific false claim about tax arbitrage makes this dimension very low.
